coredata.dc:title: Hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets as metal-free electrochemical catalysts for oxygen reduction reactions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Siyong
Chandra Mallick, Bikash
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Gandomi, Yasser Ashraf
Zhang, Ren-Shou
coredata.dc:description: A high-performance ball milling technique was developed for synthesizing hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) carbon paper (CP) electrodes as metal-free catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen storage (electrochemically) in acidic media. The h-BN nanosheets were functionalized with glycine to enhance the number of active sites and to boost the catalytic activity. The ball-milled h-BN catalytic electrodes demonstrated ultra-high catalytic activity toward electrochemical hydrogen adsorption/desorption (∼3.5 times higher than pristine electrodes) as well as ORR in acidic electrolytes. Furthermore, in-situ durability analysis of the h-BN electrodes was performed via conducting a long-duration cycling experiment (>200 cycles). A mechanistic reaction pathway (sequential) including chemisorption and charge transfer reactions (four-electron and two-electron pathways) was also proposed for the ORR. Considering superior catalytic activity of as-prepared h-BN/CP electrodes, this class of metal-free nanostructured materials can be employed as inexpensive catalysts for the electrochemical H-storage and ORR within various energy storage/conversion devices (e.g., batteries, electrolyzers, and fuel cells).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hexagonal boron nitride
Electrochemical catalysts
Oxygen reduction reaction
Catalytic activity
Two-dimensional sheets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The crystalline structure and (b) typical XRD patterns of different h-BN samples and.
(a) Survey-scan XPS spectra of different h-BN samples, and (b) C/B and (c) O/B atomic ratio as a decreasing function of ball milling speed.
XPS of pristine h-BN samples: (a) B 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) C 1s peak.
XPS of BN-400 sample: (a) B 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) C 1s peak.
HR-TEM micrographs of (a, b) pristine h-BN and (c, d) BN-200 samples. The inset of each figure shows their corresponding SAD patterns.
HR-TEM micrographs of (a, b) BN-400 and (c, d) BN-600 samples. The inset of each figure shows their corresponding SAD patterns.
High-resolution TEM micrographs of (a) pristine h-BN and (d) BN-600 samples.
SEM images for (a) pristine h-BN, (b) BN-200, (c) BN-400, and (d) BN-600 samples attached to individual carbon fiber.
Typical CV profiles of (a) pristine h-BN, (b) BN-200, (c) BN-400, and (d) BN-600 electrodes in 1 M sulfuric acid at different sweep rates under nitrogen atmosphere.

(a) Typical CV profiles of different catalyst electrodes in 1 M sulfuric acid under oxygen flow at 50 mV s−1, and (b) total charge transfer of different catalysts at 1st and 200th cycle, where Q represents total charge transfer from the catalyst electrodes.
Typical LSV profiles of (a) BN-200, (b) BN-400, and (c) BN-600 catalyst electrodes under oxygen flow at different scan rates.
Current–time curves for the ORR activity at 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl for both h-BN catalyst electrodes.

head.ce:title: Hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets as metal-free electrochemical catalysts for oxygen reduction reactions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A high-performance ball milling technique was developed for synthesizing hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) carbon paper (CP) electrodes as metal-free catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen storage (electrochemically) in acidic media. The h-BN nanosheets were functionalized with glycine to enhance the number of active sites and to boost the catalytic activity. The ball-milled h-BN catalytic electrodes demonstrated ultra-high catalytic activity toward electrochemical hydrogen adsorption/desorption (∼3.5 times higher than pristine electrodes) as well as ORR in acidic electrolytes. Furthermore, in-situ durability analysis of the h-BN electrodes was performed via conducting a long-duration cycling experiment (>200 cycles). A mechanistic reaction pathway (sequential) including chemisorption and charge transfer reactions (four-electron and two-electron pathways) was also proposed for the ORR. Considering superior catalytic activity of as-prepared h-BN/CP electrodes, this class of metal-free nanostructured materials can be employed as inexpensive catalysts for the electrochemical H-storage and ORR within various energy storage/conversion devices (e.g., batteries, electrolyzers, and fuel cells).

ce:section.ce:para: The application of two-dimensional (2D) materials for energy storage and conversion has significantly increased in recent years. White graphene (i.e., non-carbon graphene) is one of the most promising 2D materials that comprised of multiple layers of nitrogen (N) and boron (B) atoms forming a hexagonal structure. Through adjusting temperature and pressure, other crystal structures (e.g., diamond-like cubic, rhomboedral, and wurzite) can also be formed; however, the hexagonal shape is the most stable variation at ambient temperature thanks to the strong covalent bonds forming a graphene-like 2D structure [1–6]. Having a wide bandgap (5–6 eV) layered microstructure, the hexagonal boron-nitride (h-BN) demonstrates a superior chemical and thermal stability [7–11]. The bandgap structure of the h-BN monolayer can be altered through B- and N-vacancies or with the presence of any impurity within the morphology [12,13]. Indeed, significant prior works have been dedicated for functionalizing the BN nanosheets for improving the optical, electrochemical, and biochemical properties of h-BN structures [14–18].

Developing inexpensive and efficient catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is highly demanded given the key role of the ORR in various electrochemical systems (e.g., fuel cells, metal-air batteries) [19–21]. Most commonly, the precious metals (e.g., Pt and Pd) are used as the catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction [22–25]. Despite being very effective, high cost and low durability are the major issues limiting the widespread adoption of precious metals for ORR [26–29]. One low-cost alternative approach is to employ nanostructured carbon electrodes with heteroatom doping as the catalyst to facilitate the oxygen reduction reaction. In particular, the N or B single doping as well as the B-/N-co-doping within the nanocarbons has been demonstrated to be very effective for the ORR [30–33]. Due to strong electron-accepting tendency of N-containing functionalities (e.g., graphitic, pyrrolic, and pyridinic N), the N-doped nanocarbons commonly demonstrate superior catalytic activity thanks to a net positive charge imposed on the neighboring carbon atoms facilitating the adsorption of O2 atoms [34,35]. For the B-doped carbon structures, due to relatively strong electron-accepting nature of the B atoms, the oxygen atoms are directly adsorbed on the B atoms [36].

Among various N-structures, the BN nanosheets supported on the conducting materials are promising inexpensive nanomaterials for the oxygen reduction reaction [18,37]. In this work, we have employed the h-BN nanostructures deposited on carbon paper (CP) as inexpensive metal-free catalysts for ORR. To prepare the h-BN/CP electrodes, we have developed an ultra-efficient ball-milling technique to synthesize various crystalline structures. The influence of crystallinity and carbon/oxygen functionalities on the ORR activity of h-BN/CP electrodes in acidic environment has also been explored. This study paves the pathway for engineering inexpensive and robust metal-free catalytic electrodes for the oxygen reduction reaction.

To prepare the h-BN/CP electrodes, first, the glycine and h-BN (both purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) were uniformly mixed (20:1 wt ratio) inside a stainless steel container including planetary ball mills (Retsch, PM100) for 30 min under the N2 atmosphere. To assure uniform mixing, numerous stainless steel spheres (10 times more than h-BN/glycine samples) were also used inside the container during the process. Using ultra-high rotational speed enabled effectively exfoliating the h-BN samples in large scale. Indeed, the presence of the glycine facilitates the exfoliation process while protecting the h-BN particles along with preventing the formation of lattice defects.

To prepare the treated h-BN electrodes, three different rotational speeds were used including 200, 400, and 600 rpm; and the resulting samples were labeled as BN-200, BN-400, and BN-600, respectively. Upon completing the ball-milling step, the h-BN samples were mixed with distilled water and maintained at room temperature for 2 h. Followingly, the mixture was sonicated for 5 min inside an ultrasonic bath and centrifuged (at 1500 rpm) to remove bulky particles. The resulting supernatant was collected, and the sonication procedure was repeated for 5 more times ensuring the removal of excess glycine. After the ball milling-ultrasonic-centrifugation step, the production of the treated h-BN sheets was approximately 60 wt% for each sample. Finally, the h-BN samples were dehydrated at 80 °C in vacuum oven overnight.

The as-prepared h-BN samples were deposited onto a commercially available CP substrate (Toray Composites Inc.) consisted of a series of carbon fibers arranged in an irregular morphology with an average fiber diameter of ca. 8–10 μm. To prepare the catalytic electrodes, the as-prepared h-BN samples (∼100 mg) were dispersed in an aqueous solution containing distilled water (30 mL) and the Nafion® 117 ionomer (3 mL, 5 vol %, Sigma Aldrich). The resulting h-BN slurry was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min to prepare a homogeneous paste. Afterward, a sequential dipping method was employed for depositing the h-BN paste onto the CP substrate (2 × 1 cm2). The h-BN-coated CP electrodes were dried at 80 °C in a vacuum oven overnight forming the h-BN/CP electrodes.

To characterize the crystalline structure of the h-BN electrodes, an X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu Labx XRD-6000) with a Cu‒Kα emitter was employed. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy using the Fison VG ESCA210 apparatus was used for further material characterization. The recorded XPS spectra (e.g., N 1s, C 1s, and O 1s) were subsequently deconvoluted with an in-house fitting subroutine coded based on a non-liner least square algorithm with the symmetric Gaussian function. A high-resolution field-emission electron microscope (JEOL JSM-5600) and a transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, FEI Talos F200s) was also used for exploring the structural morphology of the as-prepared h-BN catalytic electrodes.

To investigate the catalytic activity of the h-BN samples, a typical three-electrode electrochemical cell was used with a platinum wire as the counter electrode, saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode (filling solution: 3 M KCl), and as-prepared h-BN working electrodes (geometric surface area of 2 × 1 cm2). The stainless steel foils were also used as the current collector attached to the h-BN samples within the three-electrode set-up. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was performed using a single-channel potentiostat (CH Instruments, CHI608) at ambient temperature. For the CV measurements, 1 M sulfuric acid was used as the supporting electrolyte and the measurements were carried out under the nitrogen atmosphere at various scan rates (30, 50, 70, 90, 110, 130, and 150 mV s−1). The ORR activity of as-prepared h-BN catalytic electrodes were analyzed through conducting a linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) experiments while flowing O2 gas at relatively high flow rate (100 ml min−1). It is worth mentioning that prior to the LSV measurement, high-purity oxygen was circulated through the three-electrode reactor for 30 min.

The structure of pristine and ball-milled h-BN samples was investigated by XRD, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). As illustrated in Fig. 1(b), the pristine h-BN sample contained one major diffraction peak at ca. 26.7°, arising from the (002) crystalline plane. Compared to the pristine sample, the intensity of the XRD spectra associated with the ball-milled h-BN powders (BN-200, BN-400, and BN-600) was significantly lower and the signal was much broader (see Fig. 1(b)) confirming the formation of a thin BN layer on the structure and vastly lowered c-axis stacking (i.e., c-direction). The interlayer distance, d (002), of graphite-like crystalline can be obtained using the Bragg's equation [38,39]. Adopting the Bragg's equation, d (002) values for the as-prepared samples were within the range of 0.331–0.333 nm; thus, the influence of ball milling on the interlayer spacing of h-BN samples is insignificant.

The recorded XRD signals were also used for assessing the average (mean) crystalline size in the c-direction (L C) based on the Debye-Scherrer's equation related to full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) in the XRD patterns [38,39]. Further details on evaluating the average crystalline size using the Debye-Scherrer's equation are available elsewhere [38,39]. Adopting the Debye-Scherrer's equation, the L C values were quantified as 15.9, 8.9, 5.7, and 4.2 nm for the pristine, BN-200, BN-400, and BN-600 samples, respectively. Followingly, the mean number of B-N atomic layer (N) was calculated based on the ratio of L C to d (002) [40]. The N values associated with the BN-200, BN-400, and BN-600 samples were 27, 17, and 12, respectively. These quantities are significantly lower as compared to the N value obtained for the pristine sample (∼48 layers). Vastly reduced L C and N values for the h-BN samples, is majorly due to the exfoliation of the layered crystals because of high-energy ball milling process. It is worth noting that the L C value decreases with increased rotational speed; thus processing at higher rotational speed enhances the layer-by-layer exfoliation level of h-BN crystals, causing reduced crystalline size.

XPS was adopted to analyze the chemical composition of as-prepared h-BN electrodes. The survey-scan XPS spectra for all samples are illustrated in Fig. 2 (a), confirming the presence of B 1s (∼190.5 eV), N 1s (∼398.5 eV), C 1s (∼284.6 eV), and O 1s (∼532.1 eV) within the h-BN crystals. As shown in Fig. 2, both the C/B (Fig. 2(b)) and O/B (Fig. 2(c)) atomic ratio decreases with increased rotational speed. The B/N atomic ratio for all the samples was within the range of 1.17–1.18 (i.e., insignificant change before and after the ball-milling treatment). The reduced C and O elements mainly originated from the stripping of both native C- and O-containing functionalities from the ball milling of the glycine along with h-BN composites. Accordingly, the ball milling procedure mechanically exfoliates and chemically purifies the h-BN sheets simultaneously. Indeed, the removal of gaseous byproducts (e.g., CO2 and H2O) throughout the ball milling process (especially at high rotational speed) leads to dramatic increase in chemical reaction rate.

High-resolution XPS spectra of B 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and C 1s for the pristine and BN-400 samples are provided in Figs. 3 and 4
, respectively. Analyzing the XPS of the B 1s peak for the pristine h-BN sample (Fig. 3(a)) reveals that the FWHM of the B 1s peak is ∼1.6 eV (i.e., similar to that of pure h-BN electrode (∼1.7 eV) [41,42]). According to Fig. 4(a), the BN-400 sample also displays an obvious B 1s peak with a FWHM of ca. 1.6 eV, revealing the presence of h-BN because of high-performance ball milling. To further analyze the XPS spectra, the B 1s signal was deconvoluted into two main signals with the corresponding peaks centered at ca. 189.6 and 190.8 eV, respectively. Considering the B 1s binding energies (i.e., 188.4 (B4C) and 189.4 eV (BC3.4)), the first peak is formed due to the formation of the B-C bond within the structure [43]. The second peak can be assigned to the B–N bond of the h-BN-like molecular morphology (sp2 B–N bonds in h-BN) [41]. Indeed, the fraction of B‒C bond in BN-400 sample was significantly reduced compared to that of the pristine one. Similar trend was also observed for other treated h-BN electrodes (BN-200 and BN-600).

Adopting a single-Gaussian fitting function, the N 1s spectra of the as-received h-BN electrodes were fitted as shown in Figs. 3(b) and 4(b). The major component appearing at ca. 397.6 eV originates from the formation of N‒B, while the minor component can be ascribed to the presence of N–C bond which has been reported to occur at ∼398.4–400 eV [41,44,45]. Analyzing the recorded XPS spectra of the h-BN treated electrodes, both the O and C elemental content decreases with increased rotational speed. Therefore, the formation of sp2 N‒B bonding is the major factor influencing the final crystalline configuration. As shown in Figs. 3(c) and 4(c), there is a minor difference in the intensity of the O 1s spectrum between the pristine h-BN and BN-400 samples. Figs. 3(d) and 4(d) illustrates high-resolution C 1s spectra of pristine and BN-400 samples, consisting of four major components including C‒B (282.3–282.8 eV), C‒C/C=C (284.6 eV), C=N/C≡N (286.2–286.5 eV), and C=O/O=C‒O or other oxides (288.2–288.7 eV) [45]. For the pristine and BN-400 samples, both C‒C/C=C and C=N/C≡N are the major bonds constituting the significant portion of the entire C-containing functional groups. Indeed, most of the C atoms within the h-BN crystals bond together to form graphene domains as evidenced by a predominant peak at ca. 284.6 eV. Furthermore, the C 1s binding energy (>285 eV) can be assigned to the C–N bond with sp2 hybridization [46], C‒N or C≡N [47] and carbon coordinated nitrogen bonds (C‒N and C=N). Lastly, the oxide functionalities (e.g., C=O and O=C‒O) are contributed from the carbon atoms bonded to oxygen atoms since the electronegativity of O atoms is higher than that of N atoms [48].

TEM micrographs of all the samples are given in Figs. 5 and 6
, incorporated with their corresponding selected area diffraction (SAD) patterns. As vividly depicted in Fig. 5, two-dimensional BN sheets tend to aggregate forming a cluster within pristine h-BN sample. According to micrographs, the BN sheets are relatively transparent with a 2D and ultra-thin nanostructure. After the ball milling, few cracks and broken films can be viewed, indicating the pulverization and exfoliation of original sheets caused by developing shear stress among the BN clusters. The thickness (in the lateral direction) of the exfoliated nanosheets is about a few hundred nanometers. The exfoliated size demonstrates a decreasing function with increased rotational speed. The SAD pattern shows several characteristic diffraction rings of layered BN sheets, where a slightly pronounced hexagonal pattern can be observed from stacking of few h-BN sheets [18]. HR-TEM micrographs of pristine and BN-600 sample at the edges of BN sheets were taken to inspect the microstructural change, as shown in Fig. 7 . At the edges, pristine BN sample contains about 50 parallel fringes corresponding to numerous stacked layers; whereas there are only limited numbers of such layers in BN-600 sample. This observation is in good agreement with the XRD analysis.

Fig. 8 contains SEM images for the pristine h-BN, BN-200, BN-400, and BN-600 samples attached to the individual carbon fibers. As shown in Fig. 8, several h-BN sheets are deposited onto the surface of carbon fibers with an average diameter of ∼8 μm. It is clear that the thickness of the h-BN sheets deposited on the carbon fibers is significantly larger for the pristine BN sample compared to the treated h-BN electrodes (i.e., only few nanosheets are stacked on the carbon substrate for the ball-milled BN samples). Herein, the Nafion® ionomer serves as a binder within the interface of the h-BN nanosheets and CP substrate while enabling the charge transfer during the charge/discharge processes.

To explore the electrochemical performance of as-prepared h-BN/CP catalytic electrodes, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were conducted. The CV tests were performed in 1 M sulfuric acid (H2SO4) solution while the nitrogen gas was flowing through the electrochemical reactor to avoid any undesired oxidation. Fig. 9 includes the cyclic voltammograms of the as-prepared electrodes recorded within the voltage range of 1 to 0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at various scan rates. Analyzing the CV spectra, two distinct regions can be identified: double-layer formation and reversible redox reaction. Based on the capacitive double-layer behavior recorded across the entire potential window, it can be concluded that heterogeneous h-BN nanosheets are electrochemically active for the formation of a double layer. The double-layer current (non-Faradaic) measured for different catalytic electrodes increased with increased rotational speed (i.e., the smaller crystalline size, the higher capacitive double-layer current). This significant enhancement is due to increased available electrochemical surface area for the formation of electric double-layer on the edges or basal plane of h-BN sheets.

It is important to note that the redox peak observed within the CV profiles (∼0.41–0.51 V region in Fig. 9) is the direct result of hydrogen adsorption/desorption onto/from the surface of the h-BN electrodes. The reversible reaction of H-storage can be formulated as:
(R1) h-BN* + H+ + e– ↔ h-BN*–Hads

Here, h-BN* represents the active sites available for the adsorption of hydrogen atoms and h-BN*–Hads represents H-adsorbed intermediates. Prior modeling studies (based on the grand canonical Monte-Carlo simulations and molecular orbital calculations) have demonstrated that the h-BN formulation can provide active sites for hydrogen storage [49–51]. At a constant sweep rate (e.g., 50 mV s−1), the peak current measured through the CV tests had the following order: BN-600 > BN-400 > BN-200 > pristine h-BN, as shown in Fig. 10 (a). This trend reveals that the ball-milled h-BN catalyst electrodes provide more active sites for reversible adsorption/desorption of hydrogen atoms, mainly originated from reducing particle size (or increasing surface area) and implanting surface functionalities (e.g., nitrogen functionalities).

To further inspect the catalytic stability of the as-prepared catalytic electrodes, the CV measurements were repeated at 50 mV s−1. The total charge transfer was obtained by integrating the measured current within the entire voltage. One comparison of total charge transfer between 1st and 200th cycle for all catalysts is given in Fig. 10(b). As shown in Fig. 10(b), the BN-600 electrode exhibits highest catalytic activity among all the samples with the total charge transfer of 3.45 times higher compared to the pristine electrode. The catalytic activity of BN-600 samples maintained as high as 95.8% even after extended cycling (>200 cycles) confirming the long-term durability. This finding demonstrates that the ball milling technique in the presence of glycine is capable of producing functionalized h-BN nanosheets that facilitate both catalytic activity and long-term durability toward electrochemical hydrogen storage. The ultra-high durability of as-prepared h-BN nanostructured materials confirms the efficacy of this class of catalytic electrodes for reversible H-adsorption/desorption reactions at the interface between the h-BN nanosheets and CP substrates.

The LSV measurements were carried out to examine the ORR activity on h-BN/CP electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 at different scan rates with the O2 gas flowing at 100 ml min−1, as shown in Fig. 11 . According to the LSV profiles (see Fig. 11) the maximal reduction current occurs at ca. 0.4 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and demonstrated the following order: BN-600 (∼180 mA g−1) > BN-400 (∼105 mA g−1) > BN-200 (∼96 mA g−1) at 150 mV s−1. This sequence is very similar to the trend observed for the electrochemical H-storage activity among various treated h-BN samples. The reaction mechanism of ORR on h-BN catalysts in the presence of acidic electrolyte can be written as [18]:
(R2) h-BN* + O2 → h-BN*−O2(ads)
(R3) h-BN*−O2(ads) + H+ + e− → h-BN*‒OOH
(R4) h-BN*−OOH(ads) → h-BN*‒O(ads) + h-BN*‒OH(ads)
(R5) h-BN* + H+ + e− → h-BN*‒OH(ads)
(R6) 2 h-BN*‒OH(ads) + 2H+ + 2 e− → 2 h-BN* + 2H2O

The aforementioned reaction steps are formulated for describing a four-electron process terminated by the water generation. Another feasible pathway for the two-electron process ending in the formation of hydrogen peroxide can be described as [18]:
(R7) h-BN* + O2 → h-BN*−O2(ads)
(R8) h-BN*−O2(ads) + H+ + e− → h-BN*‒OOH(ads)
(R9) h-BN*−OOH(ads) + H+ + e− → h-BN* + H2O2

Accordingly, the ORR catalytic cycle on the h-BN catalysts is comprised of (i) chemsorption of O2 (R2 and R7), (ii) four-electron (R3‒R6), and (iii) two-electron charge transfer steps (R8 and R9). It is clear that while the dissociation of h-BN*−OOH(ads) to h-BN*‒O(ads) and h-BN*‒OH(ads) (i.e., R4), leading to a four-electron process is not energetically favorable, the reduction of h-BN*−OOH(ads) to H2O2 is highly probable (i.e., R9).

Accordingly, the ORR catalyzed by catalyst electrodes is generally recognized as a multi-step reaction [52], in which the rate-determining step plays an important role in dominating the overall reaction rate. Pioneering studies have pointed out 4-electron pathway dominates the ORR multiple reaction steps, analyzed by rotating ring-disk electrode. The number of electrons transferred per oxygen molecule could be extracted from the Koutecky-Levich plots (K-L) equation. The K-L analysis has demonstrated that the electron number obtained is approximately 3.86–3.88 and 3.9 for N-doped graphene sheets [53] and N-doped GQDs [54], respectively. This finding is consistent with a four electron process that have been reported for other N-doped carbon materials [52,55]. Therefore, similar catalytic mechanisms could also apply to functionalized h-BN sheets investigated in the present study.

Since the ORR current dramatically increases with increased crystalline size of the h-BN nanosheets, the number of h-BN* active sites plays a crucial role in enhancing the ORR activity. The chemisorption model of oxygen on pure h-BN and C-doped BN sheets has been successfully demonstrated using the density functional theory [56]. This reveals that BN-600 catalyst provides the largest number of active sites (compared to other electrodes examined in this work) wile enhancing the active surface coverage for the oxygen chemisorption through facilitating the subsequent ORR steps (i.e., two- or four-electron pathway). It is already been shown that certain type of substrates (e.g., Au (111) and Ni (111)) can substantially improve the h-BN catalytic activity towards O−O bond within O2 and OOH formulation [18,57]. Therefore, the h-BN/Ni (111) and h-BN/Au (111) structures can serve as a robust and durable ORR catalyst.

Finally, in order to evaluate the long-term catalytic activity of the as-prepared h-BN samples toward the ORR, a chrono-amperometry experiment was performed at 0.4 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in low-pH environnement (1 M sulfuric acid). Based on the measured chronoamperometry profiles (see Fig. 12 ), due to the formation of various intermediate species (e.g., h-BN*−OOH(ads), h-BN*‒O(ads), and h-BN*‒OH(ads)) during the initial stages of ORR, a sudden drop in all potentiostatic currents was observed as a result of kinetic overpotential at low-current regions. Analyzing the steady-state current recorded for various as-prepared samples, it is clear that the BN-600 catalytic electrode provides an ultra-high and stable current profile. The analysis of chronoamperometry profiles is consistent with the insights gained through the LSV measurements. Accordingly, the BN-600 catalyst illustrates remarkable electrocatalytic activity towards the oxygen reduction reaction.

This work adopted an efficient and novel ball milling technique using h-BN nanosheets and glycine composite to synthesize high-performance metal-free catalysts for electrochemical hydrogen adsorption and oxygen reduction reaction. The ball-milled h-BN catalysts displayed ultra-high catalytic activity toward electrochemical H-adsorption/desorption with remarkable long-term durability as compared to the pristine samples. The electrochemical surface area of the treated h-BN catalyst was 3.45 times higher than the pristine electrode. The ORR catalytic activity in low-pH environment was significantly enhanced through the utilization of treated h-BN electrodes. A mechanistic reaction pathway including oxygen chemisorption, followed by possible four- and two-electron charge-transfer routes were also proposed for the ORR on h-BN electrodes. Given the superior electrochemical activity and exceptional durability of the treated h-BN catalytic samples, the design of h-BN nanosheets/CP composite electrodes demonstrated in this work can be adopted for synthesizing inexpensive yet high-performance metal-free catalyst for electrochemical hydrogen storage as well as the oxygen reduction reaction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are very thankful for the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108-2221-E-155-036-MY3). Prof. Gu also thanks partial financial support from the Talents Introduction Program of Xiamen University of Technology (YKJ19018R) and Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in Fujian Province University (IRTSTFJ).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boron nitride colloidal solutions, ultralight aerogels and freestanding membranes through one-step exfoliation and functionalization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W Lei, V.N. Mochalin, D. Liu, S. Qin, Y. Gogotsi, Y. Chen, Boron nitride colloidal solutions, ultralight aerogels and freestanding membranes through one-step exfoliation and functionalization, Nature Comm. 6 (2015) 1-8.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.R. Corrigan, F.P. Bundy, Direct transitions among the allotropic forms of boron nitride at high pressures and temperatures, J. Chem. Phys. 63 (1975) 3812-3820.
